metadata.title:
ALABAMA GOVERNOR


LEADER OF ANARCHY

ATROCITIES COM MITTED


AGAINST LIFE AND HOME BY
SOLDIERS UNDER HIS Or
DERS AND sRAvoES PAID BY


MINE OWNERS.

pages.raw_text:
L


Special Correspondence to The Press
| BIRMINGHAM, Ala, Sept. r.


The 1900 striking coal miners of
Alabama have been ordered back to


work-defeated. Maybe the ques-
tons involved may be finally set
tied by arbitration. But on the day
that the miners took up their picks
and went back to work in the inky
mines they had been whipped, and
this is the story of how they were
whipped, and why they were
whipped and of the man who
| whipped them.


Anarchy has been loose in Ala
bama. Throughout the coal mining
regions there has for weeks been a
veritable riot of lawlessness. Men
were shot, whipped and arrested.
Homes were invaded and even torn
down over the occupants heads.
Law gave way to force, and there
was no such thing as constitu-
tional protection to life, liberty or
any other incident of personal free
dom.


Most conspicuous and active in
that reign of lawlessness was the
governor of the state, D. B. Conner.
With several regiments of militia
at his back, together with about a
thousand bravoes armed by the
mining corporations, Governor
Conner rode rough shod over the
people of this community, commit
ting atrocities which, were they
committed by less powerful per-
son, would long since have brought
on him swift and condign punish
ment.


AS illustrating the condition of
anarchy which prevailed, the attack
made on a peaceful community of
miners at Republic (about 20 miles
from Birmingham) is a good ex-
ample. At this place a company of
soldiers, militiamen of Alabama,
marched on group of tents, the
temporary shelter of miners, their
wives and children, and with their
